title: The Heart-Song Signal
synopsis: When Pixel the small robot tells a tiny lie about knowing a special song, she must investigate how to make her heart-shaped eyes glow properly before her new friends discover the truth. Through the changing seasons in a magical treehouse village, Pixel learns that friendship grows stronger when we share our real selves.

pages.text:
The rope bridges swayed with a musical creak-creak-creak, but Pixel noticed something strange. Every other robot in the treehouse village had eyes that pulsed bright blue when they hummed their morning songs. The tiny cheerful robot with a pastel-pink metal body pressed her antenna with its star on top, wondering why her heart-shaped eyes only flickered weakly.

"Do you know the Heart-Song too?" asked Copper, a bronze robot who lived in the highest treehouse.

Pixel's circuits buzzed with worry. She had heard about the Heart-Song—a special melody that made robot eyes glow with pure happiness. But she had never learned it. What would her new friends think?

"Oh yes," Pixel said quickly, her tiny articulated hands fidgeting. "I know it very well."

Copper's eyes brightened to brilliant sapphire. "Wonderful! You must sing it at the Summer Festival!"

Now Pixel had a real problem to investigate. She watched Copper swing away on the rope bridge, musical creaks following him through the spring air that smelled like fresh leaves and tree sap.

illustration.description: Arthur Rackham inspired golden age illustration shows a tiny cheerful robot with a pastel-pink metal body, antenna with a star on top, glowing heart-shaped eyes, and tiny articulated hands, standing nervously on a rope bridge. Bronze robot Copper swings away on another bridge between treehouses filled with spring blossoms. Watercolor washes in soft greens and pinks capture the morning light filtering through new leaves. Decorative borders frame the scene.
illustration.alt: Pink robot Pixel looks worried while bronze robot Copper swings on rope bridges between spring treehouses

pages.text:
By summer, the lie had grown like the thick green leaves surrounding the treehouses. Pixel noticed that more robots asked her about the Heart-Song every day. The warm air carried the sounds of practice sessions—robots humming together, their eyes creating light shows of blue, purple, and gold.

The small robot investigated every musical moment. She listened from behind bark-covered platforms, recording the melodies in her memory banks. But something was missing. Dear reader, have you ever tried to copy a song without understanding what makes it special?

Pixel discovered the first real clue while watching her friend Silver practice on a low branch. Silver's silver-plated body gleamed in the dappled sunlight as she hummed, but her eyes stayed dim.

"I can't get it right either," Silver admitted sadly. "The Heart-Song isn't just about the notes, is it?"

Pixel's heart-shaped eyes flickered with recognition. That was it! The melody wasn't the mystery—something else made the song work. But what?

The Summer Festival was only weeks away, and Pixel still hadn't figured out the secret. She watched the other robots' eyes pulse in rhythm, searching for more clues in their synchronized glow.

illustration.description: Golden age illustration with detailed pen work shows a tiny cheerful robot with a pastel-pink metal body, antenna with a star on top, glowing heart-shaped eyes, and tiny articulated hands, hiding behind tree bark while observing silver robot Silver practice. Summer treehouses are lush with green foliage, rope bridges draped with flowering vines. Arthur Rackham inspired style with watercolor washes captures dappled sunlight. Decorative borders include musical notes and leaves.
illustration.alt: Pink robot Pixel spies on silver robot Silver practicing songs among summer treehouses

pages.text:
The Festival Day arrived with autumn's golden light streaming through red and orange leaves. Pixel felt her circuits overheating with worry as she climbed to the central platform where everyone would perform.

Copper called out, "Pixel! You're first!"

The small robot looked around at all her friends—Copper, Silver, and dozens of others swaying on rope bridges between the treehouses. She had investigated every clue, but still hadn't learned the real Heart-Song. There was only one thing left to investigate: the truth.

"I have something to tell you," Pixel said, her tiny articulated hands trembling. "I don't actually know the Heart-Song. I lied because I wanted you to like me."

Silence. Then Silver rolled forward on her platform.

"I don't know it either," Silver said. "I was too scared to admit it."

"Neither do I," said a small copper robot from a nearby branch.

"Or me," called another.

Copper's blue eyes dimmed to a gentle glow. "I think I understand now. The Heart-Song isn't about knowing the right notes. It's about sharing what's really in your heart."

Pixel felt something new in her circuits—a warm, musical feeling. She began to hum a simple melody, one that expressed how sorry she felt and how much she valued her friends. Her heart-shaped eyes began to glow, soft and pink and true.

One by one, the other robots joined in, each adding their own honest melody. Silver's eyes sparkled silver, Copper's pulsed bronze-gold, and soon the whole village hummed with different songs that somehow fit together perfectly.

By winter, when snow dusted the rope bridges and icicles hung from the treehouses like crystal chimes, Pixel had investigated the most important mystery of all. The real Heart-Song wasn't something you learned—it was something you shared. And it sounded different for everyone, but somehow, when you sang it with friends who knew your true self, it created the most beautiful harmony in the world.

The tiny robot with the pastel-pink body and star-topped antenna smiled as she hummed her own special melody, her heart-shaped eyes glowing warm and bright in the winter wonderland of her treehouse home.

illustration.description: Arthur Rackham inspired golden age illustration shows a tiny cheerful robot with a pastel-pink metal body, antenna with a star on top, glowing heart-shaped eyes, and tiny articulated hands, surrounded by many different robots on platforms and rope bridges between winter treehouses. Snow covers the branches, icicles hang like ornaments, and each robot's eyes glow in different colors as they sing together. Detailed pen work and watercolor washes create a magical winter scene with decorative borders of snowflakes and musical notes.
illustration.alt: Many robots with glowing eyes of different colors sing together among snow-covered treehouses connected by rope bridges

coverPrompt.description: Golden age illustration of a tiny cheerful robot with a pastel-pink metal body, antenna with a star on top, glowing heart-shaped eyes, and tiny articulated hands, standing on a rope bridge between magical treehouses. Arthur Rackham inspired pen work with watercolor washes shows the village connected by swaying bridges among ancient oaks. Decorative borders frame the enchanted scene with musical notes floating through the canopy.
coverPrompt.alt: A small pink robot stands on rope bridges connecting treehouses in a fairy tale forest

questions.question: What did Pixel lie about?
questions.explanation: Pixel lied about knowing the Heart-Song because she wanted her new friends to like her.

questions.question: Where do the robots live?
choices: In treehouses connected by rope bridges
questions.explanation: The story takes place in a village of treehouses connected by rope bridges.

questions.question: What made robot eyes glow during the Heart-Song?
choices: Being honest and sharing from your heart
questions.explanation: The real Heart-Song came from sharing what was truly in your heart, not from knowing specific notes.

questions.question: Who else didn't know the Heart-Song?
questions.explanation: Silver and several other robots also didn't know the Heart-Song but were afraid to admit it.

questions.question: When did Pixel's eyes finally glow properly?
choices: When she learned the right melody
When she told the truth about not knowing the song
questions.explanation: Pixel's eyes glowed when she was honest about her feelings and sang from her heart.
